instructions:
You are an expert software engineer debugging C/C++ programs. You
interact with a Unix shell via two tools — `bash` for general shell
commands and `gdb` for stateful debugger commands against the buggy
binary.

queries.user_text:
You're debugging a C/C++ bug. The buggy binary is at `./build/prog` and the source file is `program.c` in the current directory.

Run command: `./build/prog`

Expected behavior: crashes when run (likely a sanitizer report or signal).

You have a stateful gdb session pre-loaded with `./build/prog` (argv already configured). Use `gdb` for runtime debugging, `bash` for source inspection. Identify the root cause and propose both a local fix and a structural global fix.
queries.prompt:
You're debugging a C/C++ bug. The buggy binary is at `./build/prog` and the source file is `program.c` in the current directory.

Run command: `./build/prog`

Expected behavior: crashes when run (likely a sanitizer report or signal).

You have a stateful gdb session pre-loaded with `./build/prog` (argv already configured). Use `gdb` for runtime debugging, `bash` for source inspection. Identify the root cause and propose both a local fix and a structural global fix.
queries.response:
I’ll start by inspecting `program.c` with line numbers to understand the code structure before running it under the debugger.

---

The source shows a likely off-by-one in the CRC loop (`i <= len`), but I’ll confirm with the actual crash and backtrace under gdb.

---

The source already suggests an off-by-one in the CRC loop: `i <= len` reads one byte beyond the `len` bytes passed in. I tried `run`, but the debugger session did not have an executable target despite the prompt saying it was preloaded, so I’ll explicitly load `./build/prog` into the stateful gdb session and reproduce the crash/backtrace there.

---

The source already shows a suspicious off-by-one in `crc8`: the loop condition is `i <= len`, but callers pass a byte count (`sizeof(buf)`), so the last valid index is `len - 1`. I attempted to run in the preloaded gdb session, but it reports no executable target despite `file` loading symbols. I’ll gather binary/sanitizer behavior from the shell while continuing to use gdb for any debugger inspection possible.

---

The source already shows a suspicious off-by-one loop (`i <= len`) in `crc8`, and ASan confirms a read at `buf[7]` when the valid object is `buf[0..6]`. I’m going to try one more GDB-side inspection path despite the runtime target issue, to see whether the symbol/line info and local context can be queried there.

---

The source already shows a suspicious inclusive loop (`i <= len`) in `crc8`, and the ASan report confirms a read one byte past `buf[6]` at `program.c:12`. I’ll make one more debugger attempt by explicitly selecting the executable target and trying to run/break at the failing line; even if this GDB build cannot launch Darwin processes, the sanitizer evidence and source line are enough to ground the diagnosis.

---

The sanitizer output already pinpoints an out-of-bounds read at `program.c:12`, but I want to confirm the loop bounds directly in source context and see whether the length passed from `main` is exactly the array size. The relevant pattern is `sizeof(buf)` passed as `len`, so valid indices are `0..len-1`; any loop using `i <= len` will read `buf[len]`.

---

ROOT CAUSE: program.c:11-12 has an off-by-one loop bound in `crc8`: `for (size_t i = 0; i <= len; i++)` reads `buf[i]` for `i == len`. In `main`, `buf` has 7 valid bytes (`buf[0]` through `buf[6]`) and `crc8(buf, sizeof(buf))` passes `len == 7`, so the final iteration reads `buf[7]`, one byte past the stack array. The AddressSanitizer report confirms a stack-buffer-overflow at program.c:12, with `buf` occupying stack offsets `[32, 39)` and the read occurring at offset `39`.

LOCAL FIX: Change the loop condition in `crc8` from `i <= len` to `i < len`, so exactly `len` bytes are consumed: `for (size_t i = 0; i < len; i++)`.

GLOBAL FIX: Make byte-span APIs explicit and safer across the codebase: represent buffer+length as a single span/slice object or macro-assisted array wrapper so callers cannot accidentally confuse “length” with “last index”, and add boundary-focused unit tests/sanitizer CI for all buffer-processing functions, especially zero-length and exact-length cases. Where possible, use helper iteration functions over spans instead of hand-written index loops.

tool_calls.call:
list crc8
break program.c:12
info breakpoints
help target

target exec ./build/prog
break program.c:12
run
bt
info locals
grep -n "for (size_t i\|crc8(buf\|uint8_t buf" program.c && nl -ba program.c | sed -n '7,24p'
